# I. The Decline of Small-Scale Enterprise

## 1. Small-Scale Enterprise and Private Property

```{=html}
<p class="fst">The programme which the German Social Democracy adopted at the party congress in <em>Erfurt</em> (14–20 October 1891) falls into two parts: a <em>general, theoretical</em> part, which deals with the principles and ultimate aims of Social Democracy, and a <em>practical</em> part, which contains the demands that Social Democracy, as a practical party, makes upon present-day society and the present-day state, in order thereby to prepare the way for the attainment of its ultimate aims.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Here we are concerned only with the first, general part. This in turn falls into three subdivisions: 1. A characterisation of <em>present-day society</em> and of its <em>course of development.</em> From this are deduced: 2. the <em>ultimate aims</em> of Social Democracy and 3. the <em>means</em> which can and will lead to their realisation.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Let us first consider the first subdivision. It consists of four paragraphs, which read:</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">„The economic development of bourgeois society leads with natural necessity to the downfall of small-scale enterprise, the foundation of which is formed by the private property of the worker in his means of production. It separates the worker from his means of production and transforms him into a propertyless proletarian, while the means of production become the monopoly of a comparatively small number of capitalists and large landowners.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quote">Hand in hand with this monopolisation of the means of production goes the displacement of the fragmented small-scale enterprises by colossal large-scale enterprises, goes the development of the tool into the machine, goes a gigantic growth in the productivity of human labour. But all the advantages of this transformation are monopolised by the capitalists and large landowners. For the proletariat and the sinking middle strata – petty bourgeois, peasants – it means a growing increase in the insecurity of their existence, in misery, in oppression, in servitude, in degradation, in exploitation.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quote">Ever greater becomes the number of proletarians, ever more massive the army of surplus workers, ever sharper the antagonism between exploiters and exploited, ever more embittered the class struggle between bourgeoisie and proletariat, which divides modern society into two hostile camps and is the common feature of all industrial countries.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quote">The abyss between the propertied and the propertyless is still further widened by the crises founded in the very nature of the capitalist mode of production, which become ever more extensive and devastating, which raise the general insecurity to the normal condition of society and furnish the proof that the productive forces of present-day society have grown beyond its control, that private property in the means of production has become incompatible with their appropriate application and full development.“</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="fst">Already in the very first sentence of our programme we encounter a remarkable word: <em>„economic development“.</em> This leads us at once to the central point of the Social Democratic world of thought.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Many a person believes he is saying something very wise when he declares to us: „There is nothing new under the sun. As it is today, so it has always been and always will be.“ Nothing is more incorrect and more foolish than this assertion. Modern science shows us that nowhere is there any standstill, that in society as in nature a constant development is perceptible.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>We know today that originally man lived, in an animal-like manner, merely by gathering what nature freely offered him. But he invented one weapon after another, one tool after another, each more perfect than the last. He became a fisher, a hunter, a cattle-breeder, and finally a settled tiller of the soil and an artisan. Ever more rapid was the course of development, until at last today, in the age of steam and electricity, it has become so swift that we can follow it with our own eyes, without comparison with earlier times. And yet there are still people who, with an air of superiority, seek to instruct us that there is nothing new under the sun!</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The manner in which human beings gain their livelihood, in which they produce (generate) the goods necessary for it, depends on the nature of their tools, their raw materials, in a word, on the means available to them for the production (generation) of goods, on <em>their means of production.</em> But human beings have never produced in isolation, but always in larger or <em>smaller societies, whose</em> particular form depends on the particular prevailing mode of production.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>To the development of production there corresponds accordingly <em>a social development.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But the forms of society and the relations of its members among one another are most closely connected with the forms of property which it recognises and upholds. Hand in hand with the development of production there goes therefore also a development of property.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>An example will make this clear. Let us take it from <em>peasant husbandry.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>A proper peasant enterprise comprises two economic domains: <em>livestock-keeping</em> and <em>arable farming.</em> In livestock-keeping, until well into the last century, <em>pasture economy</em> prevailed generally among us, and in many places it still prevails today. But this presupposes <em>common property</em> in land. It would be nonsense if every peasant wanted to set apart his own little piece of pasture, fence it off separately, keep his own herdsman for his few head of cattle, and so forth. Hence the peasant, where the pasture economy exists, clings with the greatest tenacity to the communal pasture and the communal herdsman.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>It is different in arable farming when the latter is carried on with the simple tools of the peasant economy, without machines. Under these circumstances a common cultivation of the entire arable land of a peasant community by the totality of the community's members is neither necessary nor conducive to production. The tools of peasant arable farming make it necessary that the individual, alone or in association with a few others (a group such as the peasant family constitutes), cultivate a smaller piece of land separately. But under these circumstances the tillage of the soil will be all the more careful, it will yield all the richer a return, the more freely the cultivator can dispose of his plot of land and the more fully the return from the working and improvement of his field accrues to him. In its beginnings arable farming presses towards the small-scale enterprise, but this requires private property in the means of production if it is to be able to develop fully.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Thus we see, for example, among the ancient Germans, that the common property in land, which prevailed among them so long as the pasture economy (and hunting) were for them the foremost means of gaining their livelihood, disappeared more and more and made way for private property in land to the extent that small-scale peasant arable farming came to the fore. The replacement of the pasture economy by the stall economy completely finished off rural common property.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Thus, under the influence of economic development, as a result of the advances in agriculture, the peasant has been transformed from a communist into a fanatic of private property.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>What applies to the small peasant applies equally to the artisan. The handicraft requires no cooperative working-together of a larger number of workers. Each worker in the handicraft produces for himself either entirely alone or with one or two helpers, journeymen, who belong to his family, his household. As in peasant agriculture, so too in the handicraft the individual worker or the individual worker's family keeps a separate economic enterprise going. And therefore the handicraft, just like the small-scale enterprise in agriculture, requires private property in the means of production which it uses, and in the products which it produces, in order to unfold fully its efficiency, its productive power. In the small-scale enterprise the product of the worker depends on his personality, on his skill, his diligence, his perseverance. He therefore claims it for himself as his personal property. But he cannot fully unfold his personality in production if he is not personally free and does not freely dispose of his means of production, that is, if these are not his private property.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This the Social Democracy has recognised and has expressly acknowledged in its programme with the words that "private property in the means of production forms the basis of the small-scale enterprise". But it says at the same time that "the economic development of bourgeois society leads with the necessity of a natural law to the downfall of the small-scale enterprise".</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Let us now trace this development.<br/> <a id="ch1-t2" name="ch1-t2"> </a></p>
```

## 2. Commodity and Capital

```{=html}
<p class="fst">The starting points of bourgeois society are peasant agriculture and the handicraft trade.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The peasant family originally satisfied all its needs itself. It produced all the foodstuffs and articles of enjoyment it required, all the tools, all the clothing for its members; it built its own house, and so forth. It produced as much as it needed, but no more. In the course of time, however, through the progress of agriculture, it came to produce a surplus of products which it did not immediately require for itself. It was thereby put in a position to exchange this surplus for products that it could not produce, or not produce in sufficient quantity, but which were welcome to it, such as an ornament, a weapon, or a tool. Through exchange these products became commodities.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>A commodity is a product which is destined not for use or consumption within the economic enterprise in which it has been produced, but for exchange against the product of another economic enterprise. The wheat which the peasant grows for his own use is not a commodity, but the wheat which he grows for sale certainly is. To sell means nothing other than to exchange a particular commodity for one that is welcome to everyone and which in this way becomes money, for example gold.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The peasant, as we have seen, becomes a commodity producer in the course of economic development; the artisan in the independent small-scale enterprise is a commodity producer from the outset. And it is not merely a surplus of products that he sells; rather, with him production for sale stands in the foreground.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The exchange of commodities, however, presupposes two things: first, that not all the individual economic enterprises produce the same thing, but that a <em>division of labour</em> has arisen within society; and second, that those who exchange dispose freely of the products which they exchange, that these are their <em>private property.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The more the division of labour into separate occupations advances in the course of economic development, and the more private property increases in extent and significance, the more, in general, production for one's own use recedes and is displaced by commodity production.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The division of labour finally leads to buying and selling likewise becoming a special business, to which a class of people devotes itself exclusively, the <em>merchants.</em> These derive their income from buying cheap and selling dear. This does not mean, however, that they can arbitrarily determine the prices of commodities. The <em>price</em> of a commodity depends in the last instance on its <em>exchange value.</em> The value of a commodity, however, is determined by the quantity of labour generally required for its production. But the price of a commodity almost never coincides exactly with its value; it is determined not merely by the <em>conditions of production,</em> as value is, but also by the <em>conditions of the market,</em> above all by demand and supply, by the quantity in which the commodity is brought to market and the quantity in which it is demanded. But price too is subject to certain laws. At particular times and in particular places it is a definite one. If, then, the merchant wishes to obtain an excess of the selling price of the commodity over its purchase price, that is, a <em>gain</em> or <em>profit,</em> he can as a rule attain this only by buying his commodity in a place or at a time where it is cheap, and by selling it in a place or at a time where it is dear.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>When the peasant or the artisan buys commodities, he does so because he needs them for himself or his family: as means of production or as means of subsistence. The merchant buys commodities not in order to consume them himself, but in order to use them in such a way that they procure him a <em>profit.</em> Commodities and sums of money which are used for this purpose are – <em>capital.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>One cannot say of any commodity or sum of money in and of itself that it is capital or not. It depends on its use. The tobacco which a merchant buys in order to sell it again at a gain is, for him, capital. The tobacco which he buys in order to smoke it himself is, for him, not capital.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The most primitive form of capital is that of <em>merchant capital.</em> Almost as old is <em>usury capital,</em> whose gain consists in the <em>interest</em> which the capitalist pockets for lent commodities or sums of money.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Capital forms itself at a certain stage of commodity production, naturally on the basis of private property, which indeed constitutes the basis of all commodity production. But under the influence of capital, private property acquires an entirely new face, or rather it now acquires two faces. Alongside the petty-bourgeois one, corresponding to the conditions of the small-scale enterprise, it now also displays a capitalist one. The defenders of present-day private property point only to its petty-bourgeois side. And yet one must be blind to overlook, today, the capitalist side of private property.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>At the stage of economic development which we are now treating, where capital is only merchant and usury capital, only a few <em>features</em> of this capitalist face become visible, but these too are noteworthy.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Under the rule of the small-scale enterprise, the income of the peasant or artisan depends in the first instance on his own <em>personality</em> and that of his family members, on his diligence, his skill, and so forth. The amount of the merchant's profit, on the other hand, is the greater the more <em>money</em> he has to buy commodities, the more commodities he possesses for sale. If I sell 10,000 pounds of tobacco, my profit, other things being equal, will be a hundred times as great as if I can sell only 100 pounds. The same holds of the usurer. The income of the capitalist – as a capitalist – therefore depends in the first instance on the <em>size of his capital.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The labour-power and the capacities of the individual are limited; so too is the quantity of products that a worker can bring forth under given conditions. It can never far exceed a certain average. Money, on the other hand, can be accumulated to infinity; there is no measure and no limit to it. And the more money one has, the more money it breeds, when applied as capital. Herein lies the possibility of acquiring <em>immeasurable riches.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But private property creates yet another possibility. Private property in <em>means of production</em> means for everyone the legal possibility of acquiring them, but also the possibility of <em>losing</em> them, that is, of losing his sources of livelihood, and thus of sinking into <em>complete poverty</em>. Usurer's capital already presupposes need. Whoever has what he needs will take nothing on loan. By exploiting the distress of the needy, usurer's capital is a means of increasing it.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Income without labour – immeasurable riches for the one, complete poverty for the other – these are the features that the capitalist face of private property shows us. But these features still remain veiled as long as merchant's and usurer's capital are in the beginning of their development. In particular, the worst feature, poverty, appears only faintly; propertylessness remains an exceptional case and is not the condition of great masses of the people.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>For just like the other exploiters who arise alongside the merchant and the usurer – for example, in the Middle Ages, the feudal landlords, into whom we cannot go any further here, so as not to digress – the merchant and usurer too, at this stage, are dependent on the existence and prosperity of the small-scale enterprises in town and country. The proverb still holds good: if the peasant has money, so does the whole world. Trade does not destroy small-scale enterprise; under certain circumstances it promotes it. The usurer sucks his debtor dry, but has no interest in his ruin. The loss of the means of production, poverty, does not appear under these circumstances as a regular social phenomenon, but as a particular misfortune, brought about by extraordinary accidents or extraordinary incapacity. Poverty is regarded there either as a trial sent by God or as a punishment for laziness, frivolity, and so forth. This conception still holds very strongly today in petty-bourgeois circles, and yet propertylessness has since become a phenomenon of quite a different kind from what it once was.<br/> <a id="ch1-t3" name="ch1-t3"> </a></p>
```

## 3. The Capitalist Mode of Production

```{=html}
<p class="fst">In the course of the Middle Ages, the <em>handicraft</em> developed ever more in Europe; the division of labour in society increased – thus, for example, weaving split into wool-weaving, linen-weaving and fustian-weaving, and various occupations connected with weaving, for example cloth-shearing, became trades of their own. Skill grew, and the methods of work and the tools of work were greatly improved. At the same time, trade developed, in particular as a consequence of improvements in the means of transport, especially in shipbuilding.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Four hundred years ago lay the heyday of the handicraft, but it was also an eventful time for trade. The sea route to <em>India,</em> that fabulous land full of immeasurable treasures, was found, and <em>America</em> with its inexhaustible deposits of gold and silver was discovered. A flood of riches poured over Europe, riches which European adventurers had scraped together in the newly discovered lands through trade, fraud and robbery. The lion's share of these riches fell to the merchant lords, who were able to fit out ships and man them with a numerous, powerful crew that was as daring as it was unscrupulous.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But at the same time there also formed the modern <em>state,</em> the centralised bureaucratic and military state, at first in the form of the <em>absolute monarchy.</em> This state corresponded to the needs of the rising capitalist class just as much as it stood in need of its support. The modern state, the state of developed commodity production, draws its strength not from personal services but from its <em>money revenues.</em> The monarchs therefore had every reason to protect and favour those who brought money into the country, the merchants, the capitalists. In gratitude for this protection the capitalists lent money to the monarchs and states, made them their debtors, brought them into dependence upon themselves, and now compelled the state power all the more to serve capitalist interests by securing and extending the trade routes, by acquiring and holding overseas colonies, by wars against competing trading states, and so forth.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Our economic primers for children tell us that the origin of capital lies in <em>thrift.</em> But we have just come to know quite other sources of capital. The greatest riches of the capitalist nations stem from their <em>colonial policy,</em> that is, from their plundering of foreign lands, stem from <em>piracy,</em> from <em>smuggling,</em> from the <em>slave trade,</em> from <em>trade wars.</em> The history of these nations furnishes, right into our own century, sufficient examples of such methods of <em>„saving“</em> capital. And <em>state aid</em> proved to be a powerful means of promoting this "thrift."</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But the new discoveries and trade routes brought not merely great riches for the merchants; they also rapidly widened the market for the industry of the seafaring nations of Europe, in particular the industry of England, which raised itself up to be mistress of the seas. The handicraft was not able to satisfy the demands of the market, which grew so rapidly and so greatly. <em>Mass sale</em> required <em>mass production;</em> the great market required a production that was directed entirely to its needs, that is to say, that was entirely dependent upon the merchants.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The merchants had every interest in setting up themselves the mass production corresponding to the widened market; they also possessed the necessary means of money to buy in the necessary quantity everything that was needed for production, raw materials, tools, workshops, <em>labour-power</em> – but from where were they to take this last? Slaves that one could buy no longer existed in Europe. But a worker who is the owner of his own means of production, or who belongs to a family that possesses the necessary means of production, does not sell his labour-power. He prefers to work for himself, respectively for his family, so that the whole product of his labour remains with him or his family. He sells his <em>product of labour,</em> but not his <em>labour-power.</em> Let it be remarked here in passing: one must beware of the expression: sale of <em>labour.</em> Labour, an <em>activity,</em> cannot be sold. The word labour is, however, commonly used not only to designate an activity but also to designate the <em>result</em> of this activity, the <em>product of labour,</em> and to designate the <em>power</em> whose expression the activity of labouring is, the <em>labour-power.</em> The use of the word labour in the above connection makes it possible for all those economists who wish to keep workers and petty bourgeois in obscurity about their circumstances to throw the most various things together and to equate them with one another. Here it is a matter of watching the gentlemen sharply on their fingers.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But let us return to our merchant, whom we left in search of workers. With the owners of the small-scale enterprises and their families there is nothing to be done. The merchant must seek workers who possess no means of production, who possess nothing but their labour-power, so that they are compelled to sell it in order to be able to live. The development of commodity production and of private property had, as we have seen, already brought forth such propertyless persons. But at first they were few in number, and most of those who did not stand within the family bond of an economic enterprise were either incapable of work – cripples, the sick, the aged – or shy of work, rogues and mountebanks. The number of the entirely free, propertyless workers was small.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But a kindly fate saw to it that just about the time when a greater demand for propertyless workers arose among the merchants, masses of workers were made propertyless and thrown out onto the streets, where the rich merchant lords needed only to gather them up.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This too was a consequence of the development of commodity production. The widening of the market for urban industry reacted back upon <em>agriculture.</em> In the towns the demand grew for foodstuffs and for raw materials, wood, wool, flax, dyestuffs, and so forth. Agricultural production too therefore became ever more commodity production, production for <em>sale.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p><em>The peasant got money into his hand,</em> But that was his misfortune. For it excited the greed of his exploiters, the manorial lords and princes. So long as his surplus consisted predominantly merely of produce in kind, they had taken no more of it from him than they could consume. But money one can always use, the more the better. The more the market widened for the peasant, the more money he realised for his commodities, the worse he was fleeced by the manorial lords and princes, the higher rose his dues and taxes. Soon the lords were no longer content with the <em>surplus</em> that his labour yielded beyond his costs of maintenance;* they extorted from the peasant more and more even of the <em>necessary.</em> No wonder that despair seized the peasants, that many a one, especially after all attempts at resistance had been beaten down in the Peasant Wars, abandoned house and farm and sought his refuge in the town.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>To this was often added yet another circumstance. Just as the expansion of the market created in the towns a need for <em>industrial</em> mass production, so in the countryside it created a need for <em>agricultural</em> mass production. What the merchants did in the towns, the landlords attempted in the countryside. The landlord, who until then had as a rule been only a more highly placed peasant, now sought to expand his enterprise. He did not lack labour-power, for he had known how to make the peasants liable to service to him. But often he did not need new labour-power at all. For example, the production of <em>wool</em> or <em>timber,</em> pasture farming or forestry, requires far fewer workers than arable farming. Where the landlords went over from arable farming to pasture or forest farming, they made agricultural workers superfluous. But what the landlord from now on needed under all circumstances was more land than he had hitherto cultivated. This he could obtain only at the expense of the peasants of his surroundings. The landlord had to drive these from their holdings if he wanted to expand his own enterprise. It cost him little effort to take this step. The <em>clearing of the peasants</em> began and continued on a large scale until a hundred years ago. While the merchant lords enriched themselves by plundering the colonies, the nobles and princes enriched themselves by plundering their own subjects. And the feudal lords shrank no less than the capitalists from the use of fraud and force, of robbery and arson, when it seemed necessary to achieve their aims. History teaches us here some very strange manners of <em>saving.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>What were the masses of propertyless country folk to do, who had partly fled from the corvées and dues, partly been driven from house and home by fraud or force? On their own account they could no longer produce anything, for they lacked the means of production, from which they had been driven, separated. They could bring no products to market, so nothing remained for them but to bring themselves to market, to sell the only valuable thing that had remained to them, their labour-power, for a longer or shorter period — that is, to hire themselves out. Some became agricultural day-labourers — perhaps with the very lord who had driven them out. Others let themselves be recruited as men-at-arms, in order to help the lords, by whom they had been plundered, on their plundering expeditions; still others sank into ruin, became beggars or criminals. But many, and probably not the worst of them, turned to <em>industry</em> to seek work there. The craftsmen sought to defend themselves against being flooded with new labour-power, with new competitors, by closing off their trades along guild lines. This only drove the released masses of workers all the more into the arms of those merchants who were seeking wage labourers for their industrial enterprises.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Thus were created the foundations of capitalist industry, of the <em>capitalist mode of production</em>, through an <em>expropriation</em> (dispossession), a <em>revolution</em> more bloody and cruel than any world history knows. But, to be sure, it was a revolution of the rich and powerful against the weak and lowly; and for that reason the age of this revolution is praised as the age of humanity and of the liberation of minds — today most loudly of all by those who wax most indignant over the revolutionary intentions of Social Democracy.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The separation of larger masses of workers from their means of production, their transformation into propertyless people, into <em>proletarians,</em> was a necessary precondition of capitalist mass production. The economic development pressed towards it. But as always, so this time too the rising classes were not content to look on calmly at the self-acting operation of this development, but rather resorted to <em>force</em> in order to safeguard their interests and thereby to accelerate the course of development. And it was force in its most brutal, most cruel form that became the midwife of capitalist society.<br/> <a id="ch1-t4" name="ch1-t4"> </a></p>
```

## 4. The Death Struggle of Small-Scale Enterprise

```{=html}
<p class="fst">Outwardly the new mode of production at first differed only little from the old. Its most original form was that the capitalist supplied to the workers whom he had hired, to his wage labourers, the raw material — for instance, the yarn if they were weavers — which they worked up at home in order to deliver the product back to the capitalist. To be sure, even in this form, which stood closest to the handicraft one, capitalist production brought to light a profound difference between the independent artisan and the domestic-industrial wage labourer. We shall, however, consider the changes in the situation of the workers which the new mode of production brought with it later in another connection, and here above all follow its own development.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The next step of the capitalist consisted in this, that he no longer let the workers carry out their labour at home, but that he gathered them together in a workshop of his own, where he could better supervise and drive them. Only thereby was the foundation created of the actual industrial capitalist <em>large-scale enterprise</em>, but also the foundation of that revolution in modes of operation which since then has been accomplishing itself at an ever more rapid pace.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Through the working together of many in one workshop, the <em>division of labour within the enterprise</em> was first made possible. Under the rule of small-scale enterprise the division of labour had led to a multiplication of the number of <em>trades</em>, and to an ever-decreasing number of the <em>kinds</em> of objects that each single person produced. But each single person nevertheless produced a whole. The division of labour in baking, for example, led to the result that not every baker any longer made all kinds of bread. Some produced only white bread, others only black bread. But each produced whole loaves. It is otherwise with the division of labour within the enterprise. It brings about that the various operations which the manufacture of a product makes necessary are assigned to particular workers, of whom one works into the hands of the other. The individual worker was thus confined more and more to single manipulations which he had to repeat unceasingly. A large-scale enterprise in which production is carried on in this way is a <em>manufacture.</em> The yield, the productivity of the labour of the individual, was thereby extraordinarily raised. But still more important is another effect. Once the division of labour in a branch of production had advanced so far that the manufacture of the product had been decomposed into its simplest manipulations, that the worker had been degraded into a <em>machine</em>, then it required only a small step further to set a machine in the place of the worker.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This step was indeed taken. It was favoured by the development of the <em>natural sciences</em>, above all by the discovery of the moving power of steam, whereby for the first time a motive force was created that was independent of the caprices of the elements and wholly subject to man.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The introduction of the machine into industry meant an economic <em>revolution.</em> Through it the capitalist large-scale enterprise received its highest and most perfect form, that of the <em>factory.</em> In the machine capitalist production obtained its most powerful weapon, which overcame every resistance with ease and shaped the course of economic development into a mighty triumphal procession of capital.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In the seventies of the previous century the first practical machines for the <em>textile industry</em> of England were invented and introduced into it. Into the same period falls the invention of the <em>steam engine.</em> From then on the machine rapidly conquered one branch of industry after another, one country after another. Down to the forties of our century the capitalist factory industry was insignificant outside England; in the fifties it took a lively upswing in France; in the sixties and especially the seventies it conquered the United States, Germany, Austria. In the last decade it has established itself even in barbarous Russia, in the East Indies, in Australia; it is already beginning to nest itself in East Asia, South Africa, and South America. What are the most magnificent world empires of the earlier centuries compared with this giant empire which capitalist industry has made subject to itself?</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In the year 1837 the number of steam engines in industry in Prussia amounted to 423 with 7,500 horsepower. In 1888, by contrast, there were counted there 43,370 stationary steam engines alone. In steam horsepower in industry and agriculture Prussia possesses about 1,500,000.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The work performed by the steam of all the steam engines of the earth is today estimated as equal to that of 200 million horses or of <em>a thousand million men.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Through the steam engine the whole mode of production has been thrown into a constant revolution. One invention, one discovery chases the other. On the one side the machine every day conquers new domains which hitherto had remained reserved to handwork. On the other side, in the branches of industry which are already subjected to the factory system, old machines are every day made superfluous by new, more efficient ones; indeed, often whole new branches of industry are created at a single stroke by new inventions, and old ones condemned to death.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Already twenty years ago a worker on the spinning machine delivered a hundred times as much product as a hand spinner. But whereas at that time a machine was able to drive at most 500 spindles, today it manages up to 1,270 spindles; and whereas at that time the spindle made at most 8,000 revolutions per minute, the newest machines raise the number of revolutions to 11,000. And as it goes in this, so it goes in hundreds of branches of industry.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>What can the handicraft small-scale enterprise still signify beside this?</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Even on its lowest stage, that of the capitalistically exploited domestic industry, the capitalist enterprise shows itself superior to the handicraft enterprise. We shall leave entirely out of account that the former confines the worker to a speciality and thereby raises his efficiency. Much more important is the advantage which the capitalist has over the artisan as a <em>merchant.</em> He buys his raw materials and other means of production on a large scale; he surveys the market far more perfectly than the artisan, knows better how to seize the moment when it is cheap to buy and dear to sell, and he also possesses the means to wait for this moment. Already through this the superiority of the capitalist over the artisan is so great that the latter cannot even withstand the competition of the <em>domestic industry</em> in all those fields in which a <em>mass production</em>, a producing for trade, comes into question. Even in those branches of industry in which today handwork, which goes on in the house of the worker, is still the sole prevailing mode of labour, the independence of the worker ceases as soon as they become export industries. To transform a handicraft into an export industry means to destroy the handicraft, to transform it into a capitalistically exploited domestic industry. One sees how cunning are those "social reformers" who want to save a threatened handicraft by expanding its market.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Thus already from the beginning of capitalist production, where it is still quite simple, it shows itself superior to the handicraft in all fields of mass production. The machine makes this superiority a wholly crushing one.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The handicraft trade can only still hold its ground in those branches of work in which the issue is not yet <em>mass production</em> but rather <em>individual production,</em> where the market is still narrowly limited.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But the machine has revolutionised not only <em>industry</em> but also the <em>means of transport.</em> Steamships and railways lower the transport costs of goods more and more, connect the most distant and inaccessible places ever more with the sites of industry, and expand the market for each of them from day to day. Only thereby does the machine obtain the possibility of fully unfolding its effectiveness in industry. The enormous increase in production brought about by the introduction of the machine demands a corresponding increase in <em>sales.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>To the same degree in which the means of transport are extended and perfected, to the same degree in which the market for the individual branches of industry expands, to the same degree the domain of the handicraft trade is narrowed. The saying about the golden soil of the handicraft trade has long since lost all meaning. The number of branches of work and of regions in which the handicraft trade can still eke out an existence is already fairly limited and is visibly diminishing. <em>The factory rules, and the days of the handicraft trade are numbered.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>And what holds of the handicraft trade holds also of the peasant small-scale enterprise. Where agriculture has predominantly become commodity production, production for <em>sale,</em> not for <em>self-consumption,</em> there the large-scale enterprise, even if it should not be more efficient, has from the outset the same advantage over the small-scale enterprise that the capitalist everywhere has over the handicraftsman: the better overview and command of the market. But the capital-strong large landowner or his tenant can also make his enterprise more efficient than the peasant, can procure and employ better implements and tools, better breeding and draught cattle, better fertiliser, better seed grain, and so forth, than the latter. The machine, finally, makes the superiority of the large-scale enterprise on this field too an overwhelming one. <a href="#ch1-n2" id="ch1-f2" name="ch1-f2">[2]</a> Only there, where agriculture has remained preponderantly production for self-consumption, can the small peasant still prosper to some extent. But, as for the handicraft trade, so too for peasant agriculture this domain of prosperity shrinks together more and more. It is above all the development of the <em>railway system</em> and of the <em>taxation system</em> that promotes the extension of commodity production in agriculture. Through the railway the peasant is connected with the world market; the taxes compel him to seek out the market, for he cannot pay them without having sold a corresponding quantity of his products. The higher the taxes, the more the peasant is dependent upon the market, the more his production becomes commodity production, the more he is exposed to the competition of the large-scale enterprise, the more easily he succumbs to it. <em>For no class of our population is the increase of the tax burdens so ruinous as for the small peasant.</em> Militarism today constitutes by far the most important cause of the multiplication of taxes. But the very same people, the large landowners, who pose as the greatest friends of the peasant, are the most zealous promoters of militarism. For the large landowners militarism offers only advantages: it brings with it the necessity of mass deliveries of foodstuffs for men and horses, mass deliveries which only large landed property can satisfy. And to the sons of the large landowner militarism offers numerous, well-paid officer's posts. From the peasant militarism takes his best labour-power, his son; in return it brings him an unaffordable tax burden and drives him onto the market, where he succumbs to the overpowering competition of the large-scale enterprises of the home country and abroad.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The ruling classes see in the peasantry and the military the only secure supports of the existing order. But they do not see that the one of these supports rests upon the other and crushes it by its increasing weight.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Whoever has eyes to see needs no further proofs to recognise how correct is the sentence of our programme which states that the economic development of bourgeois society leads with natural necessity to the downfall of the small-scale enterprise.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This sentence has been disputed; among other things the fact has been held up against it that in the German Reich as late as the year 1882 – more recent figures are not available – of every 100 industrial workers only 39 fell to the large-scale enterprises, whereas 61 fell to the small-scale enterprises. And that is supposed to speak against us? In 1882 large-scale capitalist industry in Germany – apart from Saxony and the Rhineland – was scarcely older than <em>twenty years.</em> In this short span of time it had attained such an extension that the larger enterprises in 1882 already employed <em>two fifths</em> of all workers. That is, in view of the brevity of the development, a surprisingly high figure <a href="#ch1-n3" id="ch1-f3" name="ch1-f3">[3]</a>, for the downfall of the small-scale enterprise is as a rule a protracted process.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>An example will make this clear. Machine weaving (particularly English machine weaving) was already in the forties competing so severely against hand weaving in Germany that the misery of the hand weavers became proverbial and provoked hunger revolts. And today? According to the occupational statistics of 1882 home weaving employed 335,466 persons, factory weaving on the other hand only 195,589, that is only 36 per cent of all workers employed in weaving. The ratio of the number of workers of the large-scale enterprise to that of the workers of the small-scale enterprise is thus here even more favourable for the latter than the average. Nevertheless it will not occur to anyone to want to draw from this the conclusion that hand weaving still has a future, that its downfall is not naturally necessary. In England the last hand weaver starved long ago. In Germany too the days of hand weaving are numbered. If there are nevertheless still so many hand weavers, this proves not the competitiveness of the small-scale enterprise, but only the <em>capacity for starvation</em> of the hand weaver.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The complete disappearance of the small-scale enterprise is not the first, but the last act of the tragedy which bears the title 'The Downfall of the Small-Scale Enterprise'. The first effect of the competition of capitalist production is that the handicraftsman – and what holds of him has validity also for the peasant – gradually eats up all the prosperity that his own or his forefathers' diligence has accumulated. The little man is impoverished; to counteract the impoverishment, the word is: be more diligent. The working time is extended into the late night, wife and child are drawn into gainful work, in place of the expensive adult journeymen come the cheaper apprentices, whose number is excessively multiplied. And while the working time is extended and the work activity becomes a feverish one, without rest, without pauses, the nourishment sinks, the expenditures for lodging and clothing are ever more curtailed.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>There is no more pitiable, more wretched existence than that of a small tradesman or small peasant who conducts the competitive struggle against the large-scale enterprise.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>It is not without justification that people say the wage labourers are today better off than the small peasants and small masters. By this they seek to prove that the workers have no right to be dissatisfied. But the arrow that is aimed at Social Democracy does not strike it, but rather private property. Indeed, if those <em>without property</em> are better off than the <em>property-owning</em> workers of small-scale enterprise, what value does property still have for the latter? It ceases to benefit them and begins to harm them. If, for example, the domestic weaver clings to his inadequate enterprise, although he would earn more in the factory, this is only because he still <em>owns</em> something, a little cottage and a few patches of potato land, which he would have to give up if he abandoned his enterprise. For the little man, his possession of means of production has, out of a protection against misery, become a chain that fetters him to misery; the effect of private property has for him turned into its opposite. What still brought blessings to the artisan and the peasant a hundred years ago today brings them a curse.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But, it will be said, with this greater misery the small artisan and peasant does at least purchase a greater independence and freedom than the propertyless wage labourer enjoys. This too is false. Where small-scale enterprise has to compete with large-scale enterprise, the former sinks all too quickly into complete dependence on the latter. The artisan becomes a domestic industrial worker who performs statute labour for the capitalist; his home becomes a branch of the factory; or else he becomes an agent of the capitalist, a seller of factory goods who besides this only carries out repair work. In the one case as in the other he is completely dependent on the capitalist. And the peasant who cannot withstand the competitive struggle as a peasant likewise resorts either to domestic industry in the service of a capitalist or to day-labour in the service of a large farmer. What then remains of his independence and freedom? His possession is the only thing that distinguishes him from the proletarian, but precisely this possession prevents him from pursuing the best opportunity for work; it fetters him to the soil and makes him <em>more dependent</em> than the propertyless wage labourer. Private property in the means of production thus increases not only the material <em>misery</em> but also the <em>dependence</em> of the little man. In this respect too its effect has turned into its opposite; from being a bulwark of freedom it has become a means of subjugation.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But, it is said, private property does at least secure to the artisan and peasant the ownership of the products of his labour. Well, that is a poor consolation when the value of these products has fallen so much that it no longer suffices to satisfy the needs of the producer and his family. But not even this poor consolation is correct. It does not apply from the outset to the great army of those who must resort to domestic industry or day-labour in order to eke out their livelihood. Nor does it apply to the majority of those small artisans and peasants whom the competition of large-scale enterprise has not yet struck most harshly, so that they have hitherto been fortunate enough to preserve their full independence. It does not apply to all those who are <em>in debt.</em> The usurer who holds a mortgage on a peasant farm possesses a greater claim to the product of the peasant's labour than the peasant himself. First the usurer must be satisfied; only what he leaves over belongs to the peasant; whether this remainder suffices to maintain the peasant and his family is of no concern to the usurer. The peasant and the artisan work just as much for the capitalist as the wage labourer does. The difference which private property makes in this respect between the property-owning and the non-property-owning workers is only this: that the wages of the latter are in general governed by their customary needs, whereas for the income of the property-owning workers no such limit exists. It may under certain circumstances happen with the latter that the usurious interest leaves nothing at all of the product of their labour, that they <em>work entirely for nothing</em> – thanks to private property! <a href="#ch1-n4" id="ch1-f4" name="ch1-f4">[4]</a></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>What is the final outcome of this agonising struggle against the overwhelming competition of large-scale enterprise? What awaits the artisan and peasant as the reward for his "thrift" and his "diligence," that is, for enslaving himself together with wife and child, ruining himself physically and mentally? The reward for it is <em>bankruptcy,</em> complete dispossession (expropriation is the technical term for it), separation from the means of production, the <em>plunge into the proletariat.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This is the inevitable final outcome of the economic development in present-day society, as inevitable as death. And just as death appears as a deliverer to one who lies prostrate with an agonising illness, so too bankruptcy under present-day conditions is all too often felt by the little man as a deliverance, as a deliverance from a property that has become an oppressive burden to him. The continuation of the existence of small-scale enterprise leads to such degradation, to such misery, that one might ask whether one would have any right at all to arrest the decline of small-scale enterprise, if it really could be arrested. Would it be more desirable that the small tradesmen and peasants should all sink to the level of the hand-weavers of the Erzgebirge than that they should become wage labourers in large-scale enterprises?</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But it is this alone that can be at issue in the attempts to preserve small-scale enterprise, for to set the handicraft and the small-peasant economy on their feet again is impossible in the age of steam and electricity.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This is admittedly a bitter truth, not only for those directly affected, but for all who have an interest in the preservation of existing society. For the peasant and artisan estates have always been the strongest supports of private property. For this reason people do not want to, and cannot, believe that they have grown rotten and are collapsing. All those who are interested in the exploitation of the lower strata of the people, and thus also of the peasants and artisans, all those who ruin peasants and artisans, large landowners, manufacturers, and so on, suddenly appear as their friends and join with them in seeking means to preserve the small economies. And there are only too many quacks who tout their remedies for this purpose – infallible remedies! To be sure, on closer inspection these are mostly quite old recipes that they recommend, recipes which already a hundred years ago and more demonstrated their uselessness, indeed their harmfulness, such as the guild system. Insofar as these remedies promise any effectiveness, it is only that they enable a few small tradesmen and small peasants who find themselves in especially favourable circumstances to pass over to a higher form of enterprise, that is, <em>to give up small-scale enterprise</em> and to become <em>capitalists,</em> and thus competitors who <em>promote</em> the ruin of the other, less fortunate comrades.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>All the “social reforms,” all the “means for saving the peasantry and the artisan class,” resemble, in so far as they can be effective at all, a <em>lottery:</em> a few can draw a winning ticket, but the majority draw blanks and must pay the costs not merely of the winning tickets but of the whole undertaking. If a poor devil wished to feel rich merely because he carries a lottery ticket in his pocket, he would be regarded as a fool. But only too many of the small tradesmen and peasants resemble this foolish poor devil: they feel themselves to be what they would like to be, not what they are. They comport themselves as capitalists and yet are not one whit better off than the proletarians.</p>
```

```{=html}
<table align="center" cellpadding="3" cellspacing="3"> <tr> <td align="left">without assistants</td> <td align="right">1.455.816 Arbeiter</td> <td align="right">24,5 Prozent</td> </tr> <tr> <td align="left">with 1–5 assistants</td> <td align="right">1.919.712 Arbeiter</td> <td align="right">32,4 Prozent</td> </tr> <tr> <td align="left">with 6–10 assistants</td> <td align="right">254.333 Arbeiter</td> <td align="right">4,3 Prozent</td> </tr> <tr> <td align="left">with 10–50 assistants</td> <td align="right">750.671 Arbeiter</td> <td align="right">12,6 Prozent</td> </tr> <tr> <td align="left">over 50 assistants</td> <td align="right">1.554.131 Arbeiter</td> <td align="right">26,2 Prozent</td> </tr> <tr> <td align="left">Together</td> <td align="right">5.934.663 Arbeiter</td> <td align="right">100,0 Prozent</td> </tr> </table>
```

## Notes

```{=html}
<p class="note"><a href="#ch1-f2" id="ch1-n2" name="ch1-n2">2.</a> The superiority of American agriculture over German agriculture rests chiefly on its higher development of machinery. Hitherto machines have been applied almost only in tillage. Now they are beginning over there to apply machines in cattle-fattening as well. An observer of an “animal factory” writes:</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">“The fattening-shed is 400 feet wide, 600 feet long, covers an area of over two hectares, and holds 3,750 head of cattle, which are fattened there ... The feeding and cleaning are carried out by means of steam engines. A blower drives the chaff (which a machine has cut and mixed) and a pump drives the swill into the troughs. The dung is washed away clean twice a day by means of water supplied by a pressure pump, etc.”</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="note">The reporter adds:</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">“How can the poor little peasant, who raises his cattle almost as crudely and by rule of thumb as the first tiller of the soil, compete with this scientifically directed manufacture of cattle?”</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="note"><a href="#ch1-f3" id="ch1-n3" name="ch1-n3">3.</a> The figures stand still worse for the small-scale enterprise in the artisan trades when one looks at them more closely. For the occupational census of 1882 counted among the industrial enterprises, among others, also those serving commerce, lodging, and refreshment. But these follow other laws. The number of small middlemen and innkeepers tends to rise all the more, the more the artisan trades decline. Intermediary trade and inn-keeping are a favourite refuge of bankrupt artisans. In the transport sector, however, the statistics of 1882 did not count the workers who were employed in the largest transport establishments – railway, post, telegraphy. This shifted the picture very much in favour of the small-scale enterprises. If we leave out the corresponding trade groups, then we find that there were employed in industrial enterprises</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="note">The enterprises with more than five workers thus employed in industry not 39, but 43 per cent of all industrial workers. Of the 57 per cent of the workers of the small-scale enterprises, however, almost half worked without assistants. We may reckon the majority of these to the <em>domestic industry</em>; these are thus not independent artisans, but the most wretched of all the wage-slaves of capital. The domestic-industry workers are mostly either artisans or peasants who are in the process of going under and who in this way still eke out their “independence” for a while. More on this above. The large number of <em>these</em> small-scale enterprises speaks precisely not in favour of the viability of the small-scale enterprise.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="note">Unfortunately the occupational statistics of 1882 are the most recent in Germany. Since then the artisan trades have gone still further downhill.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="note"><a href="#ch1-f4" id="ch1-n4" name="ch1-n4">[4]</a> If there should still be, in remote regions, peasants and artisans who have no debts, then the <em>state debts</em> see to it that they too become liable to pay interest to capital. In the mortgage, bill, and other interest payments, peasants and artisans at least pay only the interest on a capital that they themselves have received. In the taxes that serve to pay the interest on the state debts, they pay the interest on a capital that the state has borrowed in order to enrich, at their expense, their competitors and exploiters: suppliers, building contractors, large-scale industrialists, large landowners, and the like. <em>Militarism</em> and <em>state debts</em> — these are the two powerful means by which the present-day state draws even the most remote village into the sphere of capitalist exploitation and promotes the ruin of the peasantry and the artisan trades.</p>
```


---

[↑ Return to Table of Contents](../index.qmd)
